summary: Deep dive into por vs para. Para is for somebody or for a purpose. Por means because of, for exchanges, and for time durations. Also reviews demonstratives (este/estos) and practices combining tenses in longer sentences.

teachings.summary: Detailed exploration of the two words for 'for'. Para = for somebody, directed to someone. Por = because of, for time durations, for exchanges. Por qué (why) literally means 'because of what'. Porque (because) means 'because of that'.
teachings.rule: Para = for someone/something (destination). Por = because of, for time periods, in multiplication. Por qué = why (because of what). Porque = because.

examples.spanish: Por esta razón, no quiero ir
examples.english: Because of this reason, I don't want to go
I'm buying it for you (destined for you)
I'm buying it because of you

teachings.notes: Lo he comprado por ti = I bought it because of you (like an ashtray for a friend who smokes). Lo he comprado para ti = I bought it for you (as a gift, they'll take it home).

teachings.summary: When talking about periods of time (for two weeks, for three days), use por, not para.
teachings.rule: Use por for time durations: por tres días (for three days)

examples.english: I will stay for three days
I would stay for three days

examples.spanish: Me quedaría por tres días, pero tengo que trabajar
examples.english: I would stay for three days, but I have to work

teachings.summary: Reviewed este/estos with the mnemonic: este has /e/ of el, estos has /os/ of los.

vocabulary.derivation: from Indo-European root meaning light; related to deity, Jupiter. Masculine despite -a ending.
